meta.title: Nephrology Practice Software | CKD RTM & Dialysis Coordination | clinIQ
meta.description: Complete guide to nephrology practice operations. $9,100/month in CKD RTM. CKD progression tracking. Dialysis access coordination. Transplant workup management.

hero.subhead: Your CKD patients already monitor symptoms and fluid balance — that's billable RTM. Your dialysis access planning needs coordination. Your transplant workups need tracking. clinIQ captures CKD RTM revenue and coordinates nephrology operations.

sections.content:
Nephrology is defined by progressive chronic disease — conditions that worsen over years, require systematic monitoring, and demand timely intervention to slow the trajectory toward dialysis or transplant. The challenge isn't diagnosing CKD. It's managing hundreds of patients at different stages of a long, slow decline while simultaneously coordinating the procedures, referrals, and workups that define transition of care.

**The RTM revenue gap:** A typical nephrology practice sees 70+ CKD stage 3–5 patients who monitor fluid intake, daily weight, and blood pressure at home between visits. Under Medicare RTM codes 98975, 98977, 98980, and 98981, this between-visit monitoring is billable — but most nephrology practices aren't billing it. At $130/patient/month, 70 active CKD patients represent $9,100/month ($109,200/year) in RTM revenue. That's not a rounding error. It's a full-time salary that's being left uncaptured.

**The progression tracking problem:** eGFR trajectories predict outcomes, but tracking eGFR trends over years requires organized longitudinal data. A patient whose eGFR drops 4 points in 12 months needs different management than one who drops 15 points. Identifying acceleration — when the rate of decline is increasing — is what separates proactive nephrology from reactive nephrology. But most practices manage CKD through episodic chart reviews rather than systematic trajectory monitoring.

**The access timing risk:** Dialysis access should be placed when eGFR approaches 15–20, not when the patient reaches ESRD. Fistulas require 3–6 months to mature. Place too late and patients start dialysis with temporary catheters — a catheter-associated bloodstream infection risk, a quality metric failure, and a patient experience problem. The timing requires knowing trajectory, not just today's eGFR.

**The transplant workup burden:** Kidney transplant workups involve 25+ items across cardiac, pulmonary, GI, infectious disease, dental, and psychosocial domains — spread over 4–6 months. Any single item that falls through the cracks delays transplant listing. Transplant centers track workup completion; practices that send disorganized, incomplete documentation lose credibility and slow the process for their patients.

Chronic kidney disease is among the most clinically and financially compelling RTM opportunities in medicine. CKD patients have medically indicated home monitoring needs, are seen frequently enough to support monthly RTM billing, and experience real clinical benefits from systematic between-visit data collection. The RTM codes were designed for exactly this population.

**What CKD patients track:** The core RTM data for CKD patients includes daily weight (the most sensitive indicator of acute fluid retention), home blood pressure readings, fluid intake logs, and symptom reports covering fatigue, appetite, lower extremity edema, shortness of breath, and pruritus. Patients with anemia track energy levels and exertional symptoms. Patients on phosphate binders report GI tolerance. Patients with OSA or cardiovascular comorbidities add sleep and cardiac symptoms. For more detail on CKD monitoring protocols, see our [CKD RTM clinical guide](/blog/ckd-rtm-billing).

**The CPT code mechanics:** 98975 (setup, one-time, ~$20–25) is billed once at enrollment. 98977 (device supply, ~$50–60/month) covers the monitoring platform. 98980 (treatment management, first 20 minutes, ~$50–70/month) is billed when clinical staff reviews 16+ days of data and spends 20 minutes on clinical management. 98981 adds $40–50 for each additional 20-minute increment. Combined, a fully enrolled CKD patient generates $120–150/month in RTM revenue — indefinitely, as long as monitoring continues.

**What clinIQ does for CKD RTM:** clinIQ's nephrology-configured RTM templates surface the right questions for the right CKD stage. Stage 3 patients get BP, weight, and symptom tracking. Stage 4 patients add dialysis education readiness assessments. Stage 5 patients add access patency monitoring and uremic symptom tracking. Alert thresholds are calibrated to CKD: weight gain >3 lbs in 48 hours triggers fluid retention alert; sustained BP >160/100 despite medication compliance triggers urgent review; new or worsening lower extremity edema with dyspnea triggers CHF vs. CKD decompensation workflow. RTM data is available pre-visit so the nephrologist arrives to the appointment with 30 days of between-visit context rather than a single set of vitals.

sections.h2: CKD Progression Tracking: From eGFR to ESRD Planning
sections.content:
Nephrology is fundamentally longitudinal. A single eGFR value tells you where a patient is today. An eGFR trajectory tells you where they're going and when they'll arrive. The difference between managing CKD reactively (responding to each lab when it lands) versus proactively (monitoring trajectory and acting before the next threshold) is the difference between patients who start dialysis with mature fistulas versus patients who start with emergency catheters.

**eGFR trajectory mechanics:** clinIQ plots every historical eGFR value against time, calculates the slope of decline, projects estimated time to eGFR <15, and detects acceleration — when the rate of decline is increasing faster than the historical trend. A patient who has been losing 2 mL/min/1.73m² per year for three years but lost 6 in the last six months has a materially different outlook than the trend line suggests. Acceleration detection surfaces these patients for urgent review before the trajectory becomes a crisis.

**CKD stage transition tracking:** clinIQ timestamps stage transitions (3a → 3b → 4 → 5), calculates time spent at each stage, and projects stage progression based on current trajectory. Stage 4 patients approaching stage 5 are flagged for nephrology education (dialysis modality counseling, access planning, transplant evaluation) at the clinically appropriate window — not after the transition has already happened.

**Lab panel monitoring:** CKD management requires tracking a complex lab panel: BMP/CMP for renal function and electrolytes, phosphorus and PTH for mineral bone disease, hemoglobin and iron studies for anemia management, urinalysis and urine protein/creatinine ratio for proteinuria trends, and lipid panel for cardiovascular risk. clinIQ tracks each of these with condition-specific thresholds — a hemoglobin of 9.5 triggers ESA management review, a PTH >300 triggers phosphate binder adjustment, hyperkalemia trends trigger dietary and medication review. The pattern across labs over time — not just today's single value — drives clinical decisions.

**Diabetic nephropathy management:** For CKD patients with diabetic etiology, clinIQ adds HbA1c trending, microalbuminuria progression, SGLT-2 inhibitor eligibility tracking (eGFR ≥20 threshold for initiation), and integration with diabetes-specific RTM data. Diabetic nephropathy is the most common cause of ESRD; systematic tracking of modifiable progression factors — glycemic control, blood pressure, proteinuria — materially changes outcomes.

sections.h2: Dialysis Access Coordination: Preventing Catheter Starts
sections.content:
Catheter-associated bloodstream infections cause an estimated 37,000 deaths per year in the United States. For dialysis patients, the risk is concentrated in the transition period between ESRD onset and mature permanent access. The evidence is unambiguous: patients who start dialysis with arteriovenous fistulas have better survival, lower infection rates, lower hospitalization rates, and better quality of life than patients who start with catheters. The intervention that makes this possible is timely access placement — and timely placement requires trajectory awareness.

**The access planning timeline:** The clinical window for access planning is specific. eGFR 20–25: initiate access discussion, assess vessel anatomy, determine AV fistula vs. graft candidacy. eGFR 15–20: refer to vascular surgery for access creation — fistulas require 3–6 months to mature and are often revised before they're usable. eGFR 10–15: expected dialysis initiation window, access should be matured and ready. eGFR <10: urgent initiation, catheter risk is highest. Patients who arrive at eGFR 8 without access placed have already missed the window.

**What clinIQ does for access coordination:** Based on each patient's eGFR trajectory, clinIQ calculates the projected access planning milestone and generates a structured workflow: vessel mapping ultrasound order, vascular surgery referral, access creation scheduling, and maturation tracking (fistula augmentation milestones, first cannulation readiness). The access planning workflow is visible on the patient's care dashboard alongside the eGFR trend — the projection and the action plan exist in the same view.

**Access maturation tracking:** After creation, fistula maturation follows a protocol — typically vein diameter ≥6mm, flow rate ≥600 mL/min, depth ≤6mm for cannulation. clinIQ tracks maturation assessments, flags fistulas that are not maturing on schedule (which often require intervention or plan revision to graft), and coordinates the fistulogram or intervention when needed. Graft coordination follows a shorter timeline but requires similar tracking through placement, the 2–4 week healing period, and first use.

**PD catheter coordination:** For patients choosing peritoneal dialysis, catheter placement requires its own timeline — placement at least 2 weeks before initiation (ideally 2–4 weeks), PD training scheduling, and adequacy monitoring. clinIQ supports PD-specific workflows including effluent monitoring, peritonitis tracking, and catheter function assessment, alongside HD access workflows for practices managing both modalities.

sections.h2: Transplant Workup Management: Systematic Tracking to Listing
sections.content:
Kidney transplant is the best treatment for ESRD — it improves survival, quality of life, and long-term outcomes compared to any form of dialysis. But the workup required to list a patient is extensive, multi-domain, and spread over months. Transplant centers evaluate hundreds of potential recipients; practices that send complete, organized documentation get their patients listed faster than practices that send piecemeal results with gaps.

**The workup component map:** A standard kidney transplant workup includes: cardiac evaluation (stress test, echocardiogram, possible cardiac catheterization for patients with CAD risk), pulmonary clearance (CXR, PFTs, sleep study if OSA history), GI clearance (colonoscopy within guidelines, GI specialist evaluation for IBD or significant GI history), dental clearance (active infection or significant periodontal disease disqualifies), infectious disease serologies (TB test, hepatitis B and C serologies, HIV, CMV, EBV, varicella), age-appropriate malignancy screening (mammogram, Pap, PSA), and psychosocial evaluation including social work assessment and financial counseling for immunosuppression adherence. Each item has a validity window — cardiac evaluation typically expires in 12 months, serologies may need updates, screening may need renewal depending on timing.

**The 25-item tracking problem:** Without systematic tracking, transplant workups stall on predictable failure modes: a stress test was ordered but never scheduled; the colonoscopy was scheduled but the prep instructions weren't sent and the patient no-showed; the dentist cleared the patient but the letter was never faxed to the transplant center. Each of these is a weeks-long delay. Three or four of them stack, and a patient who should be listed in 6 months is still waiting at month 10.

**What clinIQ does for workup management:** clinIQ maintains a complete workup checklist with item-level status tracking across six states: not started, ordered, scheduled, completed, results received, reviewed and cleared. Items that have been in a given state for longer than expected trigger aging alerts — 'Cardiac stress test ordered 18 days ago, not scheduled' surfaces for staff follow-up. Items with approaching validity expiration are flagged before they expire. The transplant center coordination view shows exactly which items are complete, which are pending, and what documentation is ready for submission.

**Waitlist maintenance:** After listing, patients require periodic reassessment to maintain active status. clinIQ tracks waitlist reassessment schedules, upcoming renewal requirements for time-limited evaluations, and any changes in clinical status that trigger interim reassessment. The goal is that no patient loses active status due to administrative oversight.

sections.h2: Prior Authorization for Nephrology: ESAs, Dialysis, and Beyond
sections.content:
Nephrology practices manage some of the highest-cost drug authorizations in medicine. ESA (erythropoiesis-stimulating agent) therapy for CKD anemia requires initial authorization and ongoing re-authorization tied to hemoglobin response data. SGLT-2 inhibitors for diabetic nephropathy and heart failure with reduced ejection fraction require authorization with diagnostic code documentation. Calcimimetics for secondary hyperparathyroidism in dialysis patients require authorization with PTH and calcium lab values. Each of these has payer-specific criteria, lab-value thresholds, and renewal timelines.

**ESA authorization mechanics:** ESA authorization typically requires documented anemia (hemoglobin <10 in CKD, with stage documentation), iron sufficiency (TSAT ≥20%, ferritin ≥100), and absence of other treatable causes. Initial authorization typically covers 6 months; re-authorization requires response documentation (hemoglobin trending toward 10–11.5, no excessive correction above 11.5). clinIQ tracks ESA authorization status, upcoming renewal dates, and assembles the hemoglobin trend data required for re-authorization submissions automatically.

**Dialysis-related authorizations:** For in-office procedures — tunneled catheter placements, AV access creation and revision — authorization requirements vary by payer and procedure type. clinIQ tracks procedure-specific auth status with the clinical documentation (access mapping results, maturation assessments, imaging) pre-attached for submission. Vascular access-related hospitalizations are expensive; timely auth for planned procedures prevents emergent access creation under worse conditions.

**Iron infusion authorization:** IV iron infusions for CKD anemia — ferric carboxymaltose, ferumoxytol, iron sucrose — require authorization from major commercial payers. Authorization criteria typically require documented iron deficiency (TSAT <20%, ferritin <100–500 depending on payer and CKD stage), prior trial of oral iron or documented oral iron intolerance, and interval between infusion courses. clinIQ tracks iron infusion authorization cycles with lab-value triggers for re-submission.

Nephrology RTM and care coordination implementation follows a structured two-week onboarding process. The complexity is in configuration — CKD stage-specific RTM templates, eGFR trajectory calculations, dialysis access planning timelines, and transplant workup checklists need to reflect how your specific practice operates — not generic nephrology workflows.

**Week 1 — Configuration:** CKD RTM templates configured by stage (3a, 3b, 4, 5, dialysis, transplant evaluation). eGFR trajectory parameters set. Dialysis access planning timeline thresholds configured. Transplant workup checklist built with practice-specific workflow steps. Authorization tracking configured for ESAs, iron infusions, and procedure-specific auth. Lab panel alert thresholds calibrated to practice protocols.

**Week 2 — Training and go-live:** Nursing staff trained on RTM enrollment workflow, monitoring review protocol, and alert response. Front desk trained on workup checklist maintenance and authorization status tracking. Nephrologists oriented to progression dashboards and pre-visit RTM summary review.

**ROI by month:**

*Month 1:* CKD progression dashboards live. Transplant workup tracking prevents the first dropped item. RTM enrollment begins — expect 20–30 patients enrolled.

*Month 3:* RTM billing reaching scale. 50–60 patients enrolled generates $6,500–7,800/month. Access planning workflow preventing first catheter start.

*Month 6:* Full RTM revenue — 70 patients × $130/month = $9,100/month. Authorization denial rate declining due to documentation consistency.

**Annual ROI summary:**

CKD RTM revenue: 70 patients × $130 × 12 = $109,200/year
ESA/iron auth denials prevented: $8,000–15,000/year
Catheter start prevention (2–3 cases): $12,000–18,000 in complication cost avoidance

Total annual value: $130,000–142,000

**Investment:** Professional tier: $499/month. Implementation: $750 one-time.

faqs.q: Which CKD stages qualify for RTM billing?
faqs.a: Any CKD stage where between-visit monitoring informs management — typically stage 3 and above. Stage 3a/3b patients with active blood pressure and proteinuria management qualify. Stage 4 and 5 patients with fluid, BP, uremic symptom, and medication compliance tracking are ideal candidates. Dialysis patients can also be enrolled for between-treatment monitoring. The key criteria are that monitoring data must be reviewed by clinical staff, with documented time and clinical decision-making.

faqs.q: How do you calculate eGFR trajectory and when does it trigger alerts?
faqs.a: clinIQ plots all historical eGFR values and calculates the slope of decline using least-squares regression across available data points, with more recent values weighted for recency. Trajectory is expressed as mL/min/1.73m² per year. Alerts fire when: (1) trajectory accelerates beyond a threshold — e.g., historical decline rate doubles over the most recent 6-month window, (2) projected time to eGFR <15 falls within the access planning window (typically 18–24 months), triggering the dialysis access workflow. Both absolute level and rate-of-change drive separate alert logic.

faqs.q: How does dialysis access coordination actually work in the software?
faqs.a: When a patient's eGFR trajectory projects ESRD within the planning window, clinIQ generates a structured access planning task list: vessel mapping ultrasound order, vascular surgery referral, access creation scheduling, and maturation tracking milestones. Each task has an owner and a due date based on the projected timeline. After access creation, clinIQ tracks maturation assessments — vein diameter, flow rates, depth — and flags fistulas not meeting maturation criteria on schedule. The access plan and eGFR trajectory are visible on the same patient dashboard.

faqs.q: How complex is the transplant workup checklist to set up?
faqs.a: clinIQ ships with a standard kidney transplant workup template based on UNOS requirements and common transplant center protocols. During implementation, you configure it to match your specific transplant center's requirements — different centers have slightly different preferences for cardiac evaluation thresholds, malignancy screening currency, and psychosocial evaluation format. Setup takes 2–3 hours of configuration time. Once built, the checklist is reusable for every new workup with status tracking and aging alerts built in.

faqs.q: Can dialysis patients be monitored with RTM between treatments?
faqs.a: Yes. Hemodialysis patients can track between-treatment symptoms — fluid accumulation, interdialytic BP, access site status, uremic symptoms. Peritoneal dialysis patients can track effluent characteristics, daily drainage volumes, weight, and peritonitis symptoms. RTM billing for dialysis patients follows the same CPT codes (98975, 98977, 98980, 98981) with the same 16-day data threshold requirement. The monitoring templates are configured for the specific risks of each dialysis modality.

faqs.q: How does clinIQ handle ESA authorization renewals?
faqs.a: clinIQ tracks ESA authorization status and renewal dates for each patient. As renewal approaches (typically 60 days out), clinIQ assembles the required documentation: hemoglobin trend over the authorization period, iron studies confirming adequacy, current diagnosis and CKD stage documentation, and any response assessment required by the payer. The renewal package is pre-built for staff review and submission. Renewals that are denied due to hemoglobin above threshold are tracked with the specific value triggering denial for appeals documentation.

faqs.q: What's the implementation timeline for a practice currently doing none of this?
faqs.a: Two weeks from contract to live. Week 1 is configuration — CKD templates, trajectory parameters, workup checklists, auth workflows. Week 2 is training and go-live. RTM enrollment begins week 2. By month 3, most practices have 50+ patients enrolled and are seeing $6,000–7,500/month in RTM revenue. Full RTM scale (70+ patients enrolled) typically occurs by month 4–5. The fastest path to revenue is a dedicated enrollment day where clinical staff enrolls all active CKD stage 3+ patients during a single half-day session.

faqs.q: How does CKD RTM interact with other chronic disease RTM billing (e.g., hypertension, diabetes)?
faqs.a: RTM can only be billed once per calendar month regardless of how many chronic conditions are being monitored. If a CKD patient also has hypertension and diabetes, all monitoring is billed under a single RTM episode using the primary diagnosis — typically CKD. The monitoring templates can capture data relevant to all three conditions simultaneously. The single monthly bill covers all conditions being monitored; you don't get additive billing per condition. This is why CKD patients with multiple comorbidities are particularly efficient to enroll — maximum monitoring value for a single billing event.

cta.headline: See CKD RTM and progression tracking in action
cta.subhead: 15-minute demo. CKD RTM billing, eGFR trajectory visualization, dialysis access planning, and transplant workup tracking.

testimonial.quote: We used to lose track of transplant workups constantly — items ordered but never scheduled, results never faxed to the transplant center. Now every item is visible with aging alerts. Patients get listed months faster.
